Metal additive manufacturing (AM) facilitates the production of geometrically complex components, yet its broader industrial use remains limited by the risk of defect formation and uncertainties in their detection, originating from the highly dynamic and hightemperature process environment.
This leads to a lower temperature gradient in the molten pool, a wider equiaxed grain zone, and refined grain structures.

These charac­ teristics enhance the strength [8,9] and high-temperature performance [10] of nickel-based superalloys, extending their potential applications in aerospace [11] and power generation sectors [12,13].

field of laser welding, typical beam shaping strategies include oscillation laser welding, annular beam configurations, and full-field power- modulated lasers [3–5].

Across the defect categories, observable raw signals support the extraction of engineered features in the time domain (RMS, variance, kurtosis), frequency domain (dominant bands, power spectral density (PSD) peaks), and time–frequency domain (wavelet or STFT transients), forming a unified basis for data-driven defect detection.
Range analysis and variance analysis are combined to reveal multi-parameter interaction mechanisms and clarify the dominant role of laser power and the significance of key interaction terms.

A Time-Frequency Domain Feature Extraction Approach Enhanced by Computer Vision for Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing Monitoring Using Fourier and Wavelet Transform.
Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-60162-0 Compositional criteria to predict columnar to equiaxed transitions in metal additive manufacturing

Efforts are focused on designing alloy compositions to achieve fully equiaxed microstructures, thereby eliminating the mechanical anisotropy commonly associated with the large columnar grains in additively manufactured alloys.

It is well established that the ability of an alloy to undergo a CET and achieve a reﬁned, equiaxed grain morphology is inﬂuenced by the alloying composition during solidiﬁcation processes such as casting1–3.
